coredata.dc:title: A Redox-induced Dual-mode Colorimetric and Fluorometric Method based on N-CDs and MnO2 for Determination of Isoniazid in Tablets and Plasma Samples
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Kuanxia
Liang, Lushan
Zhou, Xie
Tan, Weiguo
Hu, Ou
Chen, Zuanguang
coredata.dc:description: We develop a simple hydrothermal method to prepare a novel nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) originated from green carbon source Liu-bao tea and ethylene diamine. The N-CDs emits strong and stable blue fluorescence (Em = 440 nm) under the excitation wavelength of 350 nm with a quantum yield of 35%. And it is used as an excellent fluorescent output for the sensitive and visual dual-mode determination of isoniazid. The fluorescence of N-CDs is “turned off” first by manganese dioxide (MnO2) nanosheets due to inner filter effect, MnO2 nanosheets can also oxidize TMB (3,3',5,5'- tetramethylbenzidine) to blue oxTMB. Isoniazid, however, can reduce MnO2 nanosheets to Mn2+, turning on the fluorescence again. The color of the solution fades from blue to colorless because less TMB can be oxidized. Under the optimal conditions, the dual-mode method has a satisfying linear relationship ranging from 2.0 to 120.0 μM with a limit of detection of 0.7 μM (S/N = 3). And it has been applied successfully to colorimetric and fluorescent determination of isoniazid in tablets and clinical plasma samples, with recoveries ranging from 94.0% to 102.4%. The properties of N-CDs and MnO2 nanosheets were thoroughly characterized using TEM, FT-IR, XPS, AFM and fluorescence spectrophotometer, the quenching mechanism was also discussed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Inner-filter-effect
Colorimetry
Isoniazid
Carbon quantum dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Syntheses of the N-CDs and MnO2 nanosheets
Determination of isoniazid in practical samples
Determination of isoniazid in human plasma samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A. TEM image of N-CDs B. TEM image of MnO2 nanosheets C. AFM image of MnO2 nanosheets on a mica substrate D. Size distribution of N-CDs E. Size distribution of MnO2 nanosheets F. Height profile of MnO2 nanosheets along the black line in Fig. 1C G. XPS spectra of N-CDs H. FT-IR spectra of N-CDs (a) and MnO2 nanosheets (b) I. XPS spectra of MnO2 nanosheets.

A. UV absorption spectrum of MnO2 nanosheets (blue line) and the fluorescence excitation /emission spectra of N-CDs (black /red line) B. Fluorescence spectra of N-CDs (black line), N-CDs + isoniazid (red line), N-CDs + MnO2 + isoniazid(orange line), and N-CDs + MnO2 solution (purple line) under the excitation wavelength of 350 nm. C. Surface zeta potential of MnO2 nanosheets and N-CDs. D. UV absorption spectra of MnO2 nanosheets (black line), MnO2 nanosheets + TMB (red line) and TMB (blue line).

A. Effect of the concentration of MnO2 on the FL intensity changes of MnO2/N-CDs solution before and after the presence of 50 μM isoniazid B. Effect of incubation time for the formation of the IFE system(Y axis represents the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs solution before and after the addition of MnO2 nanosheets) C. Effect of reaction time between MnO2 nanosheets and isoniazid (Y axis represents the fluorescence intensity of MnO2/N-CDs solution before and after the addition of isoniazid) D. Effect of the pH on the fluorescence intensity of MnO2/N-CDs/TMB in the presence of 50 μM isoniazid E. Effects of the pH on the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs solution. (Ex = 350 nm, Em = 440 nm).

A. Fluorescence spectra of MnO2/N-CDs/TMB solution with different concentrations of isoniazid (from bottom to top: 0, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 80, 100, 120, 150, 200 μM, Ex = 350 nm) B. Linear relationship between (F-F0)/F0 and the concentration of isoniazid. (Ex = 350 nm, Em = 440 nm, F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity of MnO2/N-CDs/TMB solution before and after the addition of isoniazid respectively.)

A. Absorbance spectra of MnO2/N-CDs/TMB solution with different concentrations of isoniazid (from up to bottom: 0, 3, 5, 8, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 80, 100 μM) B. Linear relationship between Abs650 of MnO2/N-CDs/TMB solution and the concentration of isoniazid. C. Color changes of the MnO2/N-CDs/TMB solution with different concentrations of isoniazid (from left to right: 100, 80, 50, 40, 30, 20, 10, 8, 5, 3, 0 μM).

A. Fluorescent selectivity of N-CDs/MnO2/TMB solution over other potential interfering agents (I represents F-F0/F0 of the experimental group, I0 represents F-F0/F0 of the blank group, F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity of the solution before and after the addition of isoniazid respectively). B. Colorimetric selectivity of N-CDs/MnO2/TMB solution over other potential interfering agents. (Abs0 and Abs represents the Abs650 of the blank and experimental group respectively) From the left to right are: Na+, K+, Ca2+, Zn2+, glutamine, histidine, L-lysine, alanine, glucose, ascorbic acid, uric acid, GSH/NEM, cysteine/NEM, rifampicin, rifapentine, ethambutol and blank. The concentration of isoniazid used is 40 μM.
Preparation of N-doped carbon dots and dual-mode determination of isoniazid.
Summary of analytical properties in isoniazid detection of some previous works and our method.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of isoniazid in human plasma sample.

head.ce:title: A Redox-induced Dual-mode Colorimetric and Fluorometric Method based on N-CDs and MnO2 for Determination of Isoniazid in Tablets and Plasma Samples

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmaceutical Science, Sun Yat-sen University, Guangzhou 510006, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmaceutical Science, Sun Yat-sen University, Guangzhou 510006, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shenzhen Center for Chronic Disease Control, Shenzhen 518000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shenzhen Center for Chronic Disease Control

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shenzhen Center for Chronic Disease Control, Shenzhen 518000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We develop a simple hydrothermal method to prepare a novel nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) originated from green carbon source Liu-bao tea and ethylene diamine. The N-CDs emits strong and stable blue fluorescence (Em = 440 nm) under the excitation wavelength of 350 nm with a quantum yield of 35%. And it is used as an excellent fluorescent output for the sensitive and visual dual-mode determination of isoniazid. The fluorescence of N-CDs is “turned off” first by manganese dioxide (MnO2) nanosheets due to inner filter effect, MnO2 nanosheets can also oxidize TMB (3,3',5,5'- tetramethylbenzidine) to blue oxTMB. Isoniazid, however, can reduce MnO2 nanosheets to Mn2+, turning on the fluorescence again. The color of the solution fades from blue to colorless because less TMB can be oxidized. Under the optimal conditions, the dual-mode method has a satisfying linear relationship ranging from 2.0 to 120.0 μM with a limit of detection of 0.7 μM (S/N = 3). And it has been applied successfully to colorimetric and fluorescent determination of isoniazid in tablets and clinical plasma samples, with recoveries ranging from 94.0% to 102.4%. The properties of N-CDs and MnO2 nanosheets were thoroughly characterized using TEM, FT-IR, XPS, AFM and fluorescence spectrophotometer, the quenching mechanism was also discussed.

ce:section.ce:para: Tuberculosis is a serious respiratory infectious disease characterized by high lethality, which is posing great challenges and burden on public health worldwide [1]. Isoniazid is one of the most important first-line drugs against tuberculosis. However, isoniazid might lead to liver impairment and even hepatitis, and there were reports that 2%~5% of the patients who were given standard doses of isoniazid had unsatisfactory effects [2]. Besides, isoniazid has a relatively narrow therapeutic window, which makes it urgent to develop a sensitive and easy way to monitor its concentration. Researchers have made progress in developing methods for isoniazid determination in recent years. Except for the traditional high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [3], there are reports using fluorescence spectrometry [4,5], electrochemistry [6–8] and colorimetry [9]. But most of these methods require either specific molecular interactions or sophisticated decorations, which makes the analysis process quite complicated and time-consuming. Fluorescence spectrometry based on inner-filter-effect (IFE) is a burgeoning analytical strategy. As a non-energy transfer quench mechanism, it relies on the overlap spectrum between the fluorescer and the absorber [10–12]. In this case we can easily make use of the target-dependent fluorescent quench to develop sensitive “ON-OFF” methods for target determination. The challenging part is to find the target-dependent fluorescer and absorber pair.

In recent years, carbon quantum dots (CDs) have received extensive attention due to their unique properties. Besides superior optical properties, CDs are also distinguished by their facile synthesis methods, good biocompatibility and low toxicity, which makes them attractive substitutions for traditional semiconductor quantum dots [13–16]. Carbon dots from natural precursors are even more eco-friendly and cost-effective [17]. Researchers have investigated a plethora of analytical application areas making use of these carbon dots from nature. For example, orange-juice carbon dots are used for bio-imaging in cells or tissue [18], saccharomycetes-derived carbon dots for pH sensing [19], crab-shell carbon dots for tetracycline detection [20] and so on. Liubao tea is one of the most famous Chinese dark tea, characterized by microbial post-fermentation. During this ageing process, the leaves went through various chemical changes, adding a special flavour to the tea. So we consider it as an abundant carbon source from nature, and tried to obtain CDs from it.

MnO2 nanosheet is a kind of graphene-like two-dimensional material that has properties of high catalytic activity and large surface area with broad absorption spectrum. Hence it often functions as enzyme-like nanomaterial and fluorescent absorber [21]. Progress has been made in tremendous analytical methods based on MnO2 nanosheets for various substances. Jiang et al. used it as carrier of recognition and amplification probes to detect miRNA-21 in living cells [22]. Peng et al. introduced a system composed of MnO2 and OPD for sensitive fluorescence determination of alkaline phosphatase activity [23]. Based on its properties of broad absorption spectrum, it can be utilized as excellent absorber for construction of inner-filter-effect based system. On the other hand, combining with TMB, it can be an ideal colorimetric indicator.

In this study, we found the fluorescent spectra of the N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) originated from Liu-bao tea and ethylene diamine has a perfect overlap with MnO2 nanosheets. Under the excitation wavelength at 350 nm, MnO2 nanosheets can effectively quench the fluorescence of N-CDs at 440 nm due to inner filter effect(“OFF”). Moreover, MnO2 nanosheets can oxidize TMB (3,3',5,5'- tetramethylbenzidine) into oxTMB, resulting in absorbance and color changes. Based on this, we have devised a colorimetric and fluorescent dual-mode method to determine the concentration of isoniazid in tablets and human plasma samples. Isoniazid can reduce MnO2 to Mn2+, and recover the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs (“ON”). The color of the solution fades from blue to transparent at the same time. The method has specific changes in both fluorescence intensity and absorbance as the concentration of isoniazid increases. We believe such a dual-mode method would provide satisfactory sensitivity and improved accuracy for visualized analytical applications for isoniazid determination. We have applied it successfully for determination of isoniazid in pharmaceutical and clinical samples, which shows its potential for sensitive and visualized isoniazid analysis.

All the chemicals were used directly without further treatment. KMnO4、H2SO4 were purchased from Guangzhou Chemical Reagent Factory (Guangzhou, China), Sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS) was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). Ethylene diamine、isoniazid、ethambutol and N-ethylmaleimide (NEM) were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). NaCl、KCl、CaCl2·2H2O、ZnSO4·7H2O were obtained from Damao Chemical Reagent Factory (Tianjin, China). Glucose, alanine, glutamine, histidine, L-lysine, glutathione, uric acid, cysteine were all obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO USA). Isoniazid tablets were manufactured by Chengdu Jinhua Pharmaceutical Co. Ltd. (Chengdu, China). Liu-bao tea was manufactured by Wuzhou tea factory (Wuzhou, China). The clinical plasma samples were provided by Chronic Disease Control of Shenzhen, China. All experiments were performed in compliance with the relevant laws and institutional guidelines, and any experimentation with human subjects has been approved by the Ethics Committee of Hospital. The volunteers are all aware of the use of their plasmas in this experiment. All solutions were prepared with ultrapure water unless mentioned specifically (18.2 MΩ cm resistivity, Mille-Q, Millipore).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was taken by a Transmission Electron Microscopy operated at 120 kV (FEI Tecnai G2 Spirit). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was obtained from an X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (Thermo-VG Scientific, ESCALAB 250). The fluorescence spectra were obtained from a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Fluoromax-4, HORIBA Instruments Incorporated) and UV–Vis spectra were recorded using the UV–Vis spectrometer (UV-2600, Shimadzu). FT-IR spectra were obtained from a FT-IR Spectrometer (Thermo-Fisher, Nicolet NXR 9650). Size distribution and zeta potential data were obtained from a Zetasizer (Nano ZS90, Malvern Instruments Ltd). Atomic Force Microscope (Dimension Fast Scan, Bruker) was used to determine the thickness of MnO2 nanosheets under a tapping mode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Syntheses of the N-CDs and MnO2 nanosheets
ce:section.ce:para: N-CDs were synthesized via a modified hydrothermal method. First, we made a cup of tea: 100 mg Liu-bao tea was put into a beaker with 50 mL of boiling water for 15 min. After filtering and centrifuging at 3000 rpm for 5 min, we added 600 μL ethylene diamine into the supernatant. The mixture was sonicated for 5 min. Finally, the mixture was transferred into a hydrothermal kettle and heated at 180 °C for 6 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the obtained solution was centrifugated at 12 000 rpm for 5 min and filtered through a cylinder membrane filter (0.22 μm) to remove the impurities. The purified N-CDs solution was freeze-dried and quantified after dialysis. The as-prepared N-CDs were stored at 4 °C for further use. The fluorescence quantum yield was calculated using a fluorescence integrating sphere.

MnO2 nanosheets were prepared according to a literature method with slight modification [24]. For a typical procedure, SDS solution (0.1 M, 16 mL) and H2SO4 solution (0.1 M, 0.8 mL) were added into 141.6 mL of distilled water and heated at 95 °C for 15 min first. KMnO4 solution (0.05 M, 1.6 mL) was then quickly added into the above solution and heated at 85 °C for 60 min to obtain a dark brown homogeneous solution. After cooling down to room temperature, the above solution was sonicated for 5 min and centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 5 min. After rinsing with ultrapure water for 3 times, the purified MnO2 nanosheets were dried in a vacuum dryer and then quantified.

The determination of isoniazid was performed as following. An aliquot of 50 μL N-CDs solution (0.5 mg/mL) was added into 500 μL MnO2 nanosheets solution (35 μg/mL) to form homogeneous solution, then vortexed 1 min to form inner filter effect. The isoniazid standard solution (5 mM) was diluted to a series of concentrations varying from 0 to 200.0 μM. Then the above 100 μL standard isoniazid were added to the MnO2/N-CDs solution respectively. After vortexing for 2 min, 100 μL of TMB solution (4 mM) was added, then adjusted to a total volume of 1000 μL using Tris-HCl buffer (pH = 7.0). The fluorescence intensity of the solution was instantly measured by a fluorescence spectrophotometer at the excitation wavelength at 350 nm (slit = 5 nm) and the emission wavelength at 440 nm (slit = 5 nm), the absorbance peak of the solution at 650 nm was also recorded. The procedure was repeated for 3 times.

In order to verify the practicability of determining isoniazid in tablets and human plasma samples, several interfering substances that may exist in aqueous solution and human plasma were selected, including ions such as Na+ (2 mM), K+ (2 mM), Ca2+(1 mM), Zn2+(0.5 mM); amino acids such as alanine(50 μM), L-lysine(50 μM), glutamine(50 μM), histidine(50 μM) and cysteine(10 μM). Besides, chemicals such as glucose (5 mM), ascorbic acid (20 μM), uric acid (20 μM) and glutathione (20 μM); other anti-tubercular agents such as rifampicin (0.1 mM), rifapentine (0.1 mM) and ethambutol (0.1 mM) were also chosen. We used the masking agent NEM (150 μM) to block the interferences of glutathione and cysteine. The group without any of these interferences was set as blank control. We added each interference respectively to the MnO2/N-CDs/TMB solution in the presence of 40 μM isoniazid. The fluorescence intensity and absorbance of each group were recorded under the same condition as above and repeated for 3 times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of isoniazid in practical samples
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation of tablet sample was completed as following. One tablet of isoniazid was grounded, then dissolved with 50 mL distilled water and diluted to a proper concentration (about 10–70 μM), the isoniazid solution was then pretreated with 0.45 μm cyclinder membrane to obtain clear solution. Then the prepared sample was added to the MnO2/N-CDs/TMB solution to analyze using the same procedure mentioned above.

The human whole blood samples were collected by Chronic Disease Control of Shenzhen. The plasma sample were obtained immediately after the blood draw by centrifugation at 3000 rpm for 5 min, and stored at −20 °C for further use. At the beginning of the process, standard isoniazid (20 μM and 50 μM) was spiked into the plasma samples, then acetonitrile was added to remove proteins. After vortexing for 1 min, we obtained the supernatant by centrifugation at 4000 rpm for 15 min. NEM (150 μM) was then added to the plasma to block the interference of sulfhydryl substances, followed by the same procedure mentioned above.

To verify the morphology and size of the N-CDs and MnO2 nanosheets directly, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed. As shown in Fig. 1 -A, N-CDs were mostly nano-size spheres with sizes ranging from 1.5 to 4.5 nm (Fig. 1-D). The image of N-CDs showed superior dispersion without any obvious aggregation. The quantum yield of the N-CDs was calculated to be 35% by FluorEssence through a fluorescence integrating sphere. On the other hand, MnO2 nanosheets (Fig. 1-B) were characterized as typical two-dimensional nanosheets with thin and flexible surfaces. The average lateral dimension of the nanosheets was estimated to be about 200 nm (Fig. 1-E). The atomic force microscope (AFM) image displayed the 2D flake structure of MnO2 (Fig. 1-C) and the thickness was approximately 1.3 nm (Fig. 1-F), confirming the formation of the single-layer nanosheet structure [24]. The surface composition and elemental analysis of the as-prepared N-CDs and MnO2 nanosheets were carried out further by XPS. Fig. 1-G showed three characteristic peaks at 284.8, 400.3 and 531.8 eV, corresponding to the O1s, N1s and C1s peaks, respectively, which confirmed the existence of C, N and O. In the meanwhile, in Fig. 1-I, there were 2 peaks at 642.2 and 653.9 eV originating from Mn2p3/2 and Mn2p1/2, which indicated the existence of MnO2 [21]. The experimental results indicated that the N-CDs and MnO2 nanosheets have been successfully synthesized. FT-IR were also used to characterize the surface composition of N-CDs and MnO2 nanosheets (Fig. 1-H). Spectrum b of Fig. 1-H represented MnO2 nanosheets, the peak at 3450 cm−1 resulted from O–H stretching of the strongly absorbed water on the surface. The peak at around 2935 cm−1 and 1650 cm−1 came from C–H and C=O stretching vibrations respectively. The peaks at 1467 cm−1 and 1245 cm−1 represented C-C and C–O. The characteristic peaks at 580 cm-1and 430 cm−1 were inferred to Mn-O lattice vibration. Spectrum a of Fig. 1-H represented N-CDs, a broad peak at around 3400 cm−1 could be observed, which could be attributed to the characteristic absorption band of the O-H or N–H stretching vibration mode[16]. The sharp peak at 1646 cm−1 and 1162 cm−1 were for the characteristic surface carboxyl groups C = O and C-OH stretching vibrations from the organic acid from tea. The peak at 1297 cm−1 represented C-N vibration, showing the successful doping of nitrogen. C–C stretching vibrations also appeared at around 1456 cm−1. The peak at 775 cm−1 originated from aromatic C-H, which might come from the polyphenols in tea.

We further investigated the effect of the concentration of MnO2 nanosheets on isoniazid determination. We added various concentrations of MnO2 nanosheets (0–280 μg/mL) into N-CDs solution (0.5 mg/mL), the fluorescence (FL) intensity before(F0) and after(F) the addition of 50 μM isoniazid was recorded. As illustrated in Fig. 3 -A, at first, the amount of MnO2 nanosheets was insufficient comparing to that of isoniazid. Thus, there is no obvious FL intensity change. With the ratio of the MnO2 nanosheets increasing, the FL intensity ratio of (F-F0)/F0 increased obviously and reached a peak at 35 μg/mL. Continuous addition of MnO2 didn’t enlarge but decrease the value of (F-F0)/F0, because the overdose of MnO2 nanosheets would make the FL changes undetectable. In this case, 35 μg/mL MnO2 nanosheets was selected as the optimum concentration.

The effect of time on the method was also investigated. As shown in Fig. 3-B and Fig. 3-C, after the N-CDs and MnO2 nanosheets solution were mixed, inner filter effect happened within a short time (less than 3 min), leading to the obvious decreases of FL intensity of N-CDs solution. Ultimately, an interval of 3 min was chosen for mixture. After the addition of isoniazid, the FL intensity of MnO2/N-CDs solution increased obviously within 2 min, which meant that the redox reaction also happened quickly. Therefore, 2 min reaction time was chosen after the addition of isoniazid for determination.

The influences of pH were also investigated. We analysed various pH values ranging from 4.0 to 10.0 in Tris-HCl buffer. It is notable that slight difference was observed in the above pH area for the MnO2/N-CDs/TMB determination system (Fig. 3-D). This result suggested that the method could adapt to different pH sensing conditions. The method performed a little better in neutral and alkaline conditions than the acid ones, it might due to the reduction ability of isoniazid increased in neutral and alkaline medium[25]. The pH-independent manner of the N-CDs solution may attribute to the stable fluorescence properties of N-CDs (Fig. 3-E). Finally, pH 7.0 was used for further determination.

The fluorescence responses of the MnO2/N-CDs/TMB solution to isoniazid was then studied. The fluorescence spectra of the MnO2/N-CDs/TMB solution in the presence of various concentrations of isoniazid were recorded. As illustrated in Fig. 4 -A, the fluorescence (FL) intensity of the solution was weak in the absence of isoniazid due to inner filter effect. With the increasing concentrations of isoniazid, an upward tendency in FL intensity could be observed. As showed in Fig. 4-B, we recorded the FL intensity before (F0) and after(F) the addition of isoniazid, and found a satisfying linear relationship between F-F0/F0 and the concentration of isoniazid ranging from 2.0 to 120.0 μM, the linear equation is (F-F0)/F0 = 0.0356 × Cisoniazid + 0.4645 (R2 = 0.9964) and the limit of detection (LOD) for isoniazid was calculated to be 0.7 μM (S/N = 3). The results indicated this fluorescent output could be used for isoniazid quantification.

On the other hand, as the content of isoniazid increases, more MnO2 nanosheets were disintegrated and less TMB could be oxidized into oxTMB. As shown in Fig. 5 -A, the absorbance of MnO2/N-CDs/TMB solution at 650 nm decreases with the increasing concentration of isoniazid, causing the change of colour from blue to transparent (Fig. 5-C). We also found a satisfying linear relationship (Fig. 5-B) between the absorbance of the solution at 650 nm (Abs650) and the concentration of isoniazid ranging from 3.0 to 50.0 μM, and it follows the equal: Abs650 = -0.0266 × Cisoniazid + 1.6391 (R2 = 0.9954), with the LOD of 1.6 μM (S/N = 3). The results also indicated the colorimetric output could be used for visualized isoniazid quantification.

The method has been compared with other previous reported works for the determination of isoniazid, the analytical properties were concluded in Table 1 . The result shows that our method has a wider linear range than most of others.

Selectivity is a paramount factor to evaluate the applicability of a proposed method. Based on the main purpose of this method, we tested the selectivity by adding a series of potential interference in human plasma. Tuberculosis patients always receive a medical treatment including other antitubercular agents such as ethambutol, rifapentine and rifampicin. Therefore, we also tested the effects of these drugs. From the results, it was indicated that there was negligible interference of other anti-tubercular agents for the proposed method (Fig. 6 ). The main ions (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Zn2+), amino acids (alanine, L-lysine, glutamine, histidine) and glucose displayed no obvious interferences in both fluorescent and colorimetric outputs. After adding NEM (a masking agent for sulfhydryl groups chemicals), the interference effects of GSH (glutathione) and cysteine were blocked so that less changes were observed in the FL intensity. Ascorbic acid (AA) showed interference to the method, an increase (6.42%) in FL intensity was detected, but the concentrations of AA in human plasma were much lower than that of isoniazid [27,28]. The addition of the remaining interference showed that the FL intensity has increased by less than 5%, so these interferences did not actually affect the response. Those results showed satisfying selectivity for the analyses of isoniazid.

The prepared solution was first diluted to a proper concentration, and then analyzed using the above method. The analytical results were showed in the Table 1. According to the Pharmacopoeia of the People's Republic of China (2015), isoniazid tablets products should contain 95.0–105.0% of isoniazid as what it claimed. We also used HPLC method suggested in the Pharmacopoeia to determine isoniazid for comparison (Table 2 ). The results indicated that the tablets products met the standards and matched with the outcomes of HPLC. The recovery tests also proved the practicability of the method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of isoniazid in human plasma samples
ce:section.ce:para: It’s often challenging for a developed method to be used in clinical samples because of the unknown interferences. To verify the clinical feasibility, we applied the method to healthy human plasma with spiked standard isoniazid (Table 3 ). From the experimental results, recoveries and relative standard deviations (RSDs) for isoniazid analysis have achieved satisfactory results. HPLC method was also applied to determine isoniazid for comparison. The results indicated good reliability and repeatability of the proposed method. Thus, the assay has great potential for the quantitative analysis of isoniazid in human plasma samples.

We have developed a colorimetric and fluorescent method to determine isoniazid in tablets and human plasma samples. Based on MnO2/N-CDs/TMB solution system, the method can provide dual-mode outputs at the same time. The fluorescent output is based on inner-filter-effect between MnO2 and N-CDs, and the colorimetric output is based on catalytical reaction between MnO2 and TMB. In the presence of isoniazid, the fluorescent output turns on, while the colorimetric output turns off. The corresponding fluorescent linearity ranges from 2.0 to 120.0 μM, and the colorimetric linearity from 3.0 to 50.0 μM. The limit of detection (LOD) of this method is 0.7 μM (S/N = 3), with recoveries ranging from 94.0% to 102.4%. Combining the colorimetric with fluorescent output, it has the potential of visualizing the whole process in clinical analyses, which is helpful in point-of-care detection. It has achieved satisfying results in complicated situations. The method requires less sample pretreatment procedures comparing with traditional HPLC method. Besides, it’s relatively easier to assemble such systems than electrochemical method, which often needs complicated surface decorations. However, there are still challenges for developing methods with wider application for drugs that have no redox properties. Future efforts should also be made to enlarge the linearity range of the method, so that it can be used in trace or mass analyses.

Kuanxia Ma: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Lushan Liang: Investigation, Writing - review & editing. Xie Zhou: Writing - review & editing. Weiguo Tan: Resources. Ou Hu: Writing - review & editing. Zuanguang Chen: Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.21675177), and the Science and Technology Planning Project of Guangzhou (No.202002020084).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tuberculosis
Lancet
